concept: Thing Classifier / Detached or Crafted Object Category
description: The optional classifier for detached and crafted physical objects: a stone, fallen branch, book, tool, building, or vessel. It does not serve as a universal classifier for water, rivers, clouds, places, or all physical matter. 'Themo' distinguishes its object category from living beings ('lipha') and abstractions or events ('nophe').
sound_symbolism: The word begins with fricative 'the', which can suggest attention settling on a boundary, and moves into grounded nasal, rounded 'mo', which can suggest a bounded physical object. The image is mnemonic rather than a claim that articulation creates conscious engagement with matter.
grammatical_notes: Functions as an optional classifier between a numeral and a detached or crafted object noun. Examples: 'ta themo shelu' (one book) and 'wi shao themo wiru' (six baskets). 'Wia' may also precede it in a quantity question. Ordinary quantifiers modify the noun directly and do not take a classifier. A living part uses 'lipha' while attached to a living being and may use 'themo' once detached or crafted. Never use 'themo' for a living being or abstract event. A numeral or quantifier already marks quantity, so the phrase never also takes plural 'lo'.

pillars.solarpunk-values: Choosing 'themo' can foreground a crafted or detached object's material status. Repair, reuse, provenance, and sustainable treatment still require their own claims and practices.
pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: An explicit object category can support attention to the physical things in use. The classifier does not make attention mindful or determine whether a river, substance, or living body should be analyzed as an object.
pillars.art-nouveau-aesthetics: Natural materials and crafted objects both participate in Art Nouveau design, but 'themo' classifies bounded detached or crafted things rather than unifying the whole material world.
pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: Craft traditions attend closely to materials and tools. 'Themo' can mark the resulting object category without attributing one material ontology to traditional cultures.
